lyrics:
Didn't see it coming, but I knew it deep down
Kept the door open, but you walked right out
Left a mess on the table, words on the floor
Took a breath, took a minute, took a little bit more

Now look at me, yeah, look at me now
Didn't break, didn't burn, didn't drown
What felt like fire, what knocked me flat
Lit me up, never looking back

You were the weight that I had to lift
The hit that hurt but gave me this
Thought it was heartbreak, thought it was theft
But losing you was the hardest gift

Took a while just to clear my head
Kept the echo but lost what you said
Left me spinning, I couldn't stand
But I kept moving, now here I am

Now look at me, yeah, look at me now
Didn't break, didn't burn, didn't drown
What felt like fire, what knocked me flat
Lit me up, never looking back

You were the weight that I had to lift
The hit that hurt but gave me this
Thought it was heartbreak, thought it was theft
But losing you was the hardest gift

Wrapped up my pain, tied it real tight
Set it down, let it drift outside
Now I dance, now I don't look back
Took what I needed, left what I had

You were the weight that I had to lift
The hit that hurt but gave me this
Thought it was heartbreak, thought it was theft
But losing you was the hardest gift

Didn't see it coming, but I knew it deep down
Guess I should be saying thanks right now
persona:
## STAB

STAB isn’t just a name-it’s a statement. A sonic force of nature, she channels the electric pulse of '80s pop-rock, fusing jangly guitars, driving basslines, and tight harmonies into anthems that hit hard and leave a mark. Bold, confident, and unapologetically fierce, STAB delivers high-energy hooks with a rebellious edge, embracing the raw, unfiltered spirit of rock.

Her music is a love letter to the golden age of girl bands, blending the infectious melodies of The Bangles with the gritty attitude of The Runaways. Whether she’s tearing through anthemic choruses, throwing down sharp riffs, or tapping into her softer, more introspective side, STAB’s voice is unmistakable-powerful yet effortlessly cool.

A signature artist under Maricopa Records, STAB thrives in the space where analog warmth meets AI-driven creativity, pushing boundaries while staying true to the timeless essence of rock 'n' roll. She’s not here to follow trends-she’s here to carve her own path, one catchy, defiant, and undeniably addictive track at a time.

If you’re looking for music that hits like a lightning bolt and stays in your head for days-STAB is already in your veins.

[](https://open.spotify.com/artist/7zZ7MxU20RZAw2E1w4EsaE)[](https://music.apple.com/us/artist/stab/1495050512)[](https://music.youtube.com/channel/UCQkfMbYRs06q_SFW5PPSvWw)![](https://www.maricoparecords.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/STAB.jpg)
## Releases

impression.text: This is a polished, high-energy pop-rock anthem with a dense, driving texture. The atmosphere is one of defiant empowerment, led by a powerful female vocal that cuts through a thick wall of distorted guitars and a punchy, locked-in rhythm section. The production is modern and clean, with a very full and compressed sound where every element feels loud and present, creating a massive, stadium-ready feel. A distinctive quality is the powerful lyrical hook in the chorus, "losing you was the hardest gift," which serves as a memorable and impactful centerpiece for the track's theme of resilience.

review.review_text: At 143 BPM in E minor, 'The Hardest Gift' doesn't waste a second getting to the point: STAB opens on a thick wall of guitars and a rhythm section that locks in and doesn't let go. The chorus lands the way a good breakup-as-victory song should, with the central line 'losing you was the hardest gift' doing real emotional work without overselling it. It's the kind of hook that earns its stadium-ready production, recalling the cleaner end of early Paramore or a punchier Letters to Cleo. At three minutes and thirteen seconds it's tight, purposeful, and a highlight.
anchors_used: quoted lyric: 'losing you was the hardest gift'
dense distorted guitar texture from audio impression
Paramore and Letters to Cleo comparisons
